EN: Meet the Bamboo Shark—a small, bottom-dweller in warm, shallow Indo-Pacific waters. They grow up to three feet long. with slender bodies and unique, banded markings that resemble bamboo stalks.
Bamboo Sharks hunt at night for crustaceans, small fish, and worms on the seafloor. Their camouflage helps them blend into sandy or rocky habitats, making them expert ambush predators.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Longfin Bannerfish is found in the tropical reefs of the Indo-Pacific. They grow up to 9 inches long and are often spotted in pairs or small groups, gracefully gliding through the water.
With bold black and white stripes and long trailing dorsal fins, they’re hard to miss. These omnivores feed on plankton and small invertebrates, playing a colorful role in the reef's food chain.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
Meet the Barrel Jellyfish, one of the largest jellyfish in the ocean! Found mostly in the northeast Atlantic and Mediterranean Sea, they can grow up to 3 feet wide - about the size of a dustbin lid!

They feed on plankton and small fish, drifting along with frilly, purple-tinted arms, and a smooth, dome-shaped bell that’s white or pale blue with soft purple markings - easy to spot and hard to forget.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Basking Shark—second largest fish in the world! Found in cool waters like the North Atlantic and around the UK, these gentle giants can grow over 30 feet long and are totally harmless to humans.

They cruise with their mouths wide open, filtering plankton as they go. With a cone-shaped snout, gill slits that nearly circle their heads, and grayish skin with mottled patches—they’re hard to miss!
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Bicolor Angelfish is a tropical beauty on Indo-Pacific reefs from East Africa to the Solomon Islands. It grows up to 6 inches long and stands out with its bold yellow front and deep blue back.
It’s an omnivore, feeding on algae, sponges, and tiny invertebrates. With its sharp color split and curious nature, the Bicolor Angelfish adds a splash of color and personality to the reef!
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
Meet the Juvenile Purple Tang, found in coral-rich waters of the Red Sea and Arabian Sea. These striking swimmers grow up to 10 inches, with deep purple bodies and vibrant yellow tails.
As juveniles, they show vivid blue hues and faint body lines. Being herbivores and grazing on algae all day long, they help keep reefs clean and ecosystems in balance

The Adult Purple Tang, a vibrant reef fish native to the Red Sea and Western Indian Ocean, grows up to 10 inches long and stands out with its deep purple body and bold yellow tail.
Purple Tangs are important grazers, and help keep reefs clean. Their sleek bodies and sharp markings make them one of the ocean’s most striking residents
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Hawaiian Butterflyfish is a reef icon, found only in the waters of the Hawaiian Islands. It reaches about 6 inches in length and glides gracefully over reefs in pairs or small groups.
Feeding mostly on coral polyps and small invertebrates, it’s easily recognized by its bright yellow body, dark eye stripe, and bold black spot near the tail — nature’s perfect disguise.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The beautiful Sohal Surgeonfish — a reef dweller native to the Red Sea. Up to 16 inches long, it’s one of the largest surgeonfish species, known for its speed and agility.
It feeds mainly on algae, grazing constantly. Look for its bold horizontal stripes and the sharp, scalpel-like spines near its tail — both beautiful and built for defense.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Mauritius Hogfish,  a rare beauty found in only three locations in the western Indian Ocean. Growing up to 10 inches long, it’s known for its elongated body and stunning color patterns.
This carnivore feeds on small crustaceans and invertebrates, using its sharp snout to hunt for prey. Look for bold red stripes and yellow highlights that make this hogfish truly unforgettable!
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
Sailfin Tangs are striking reef fish found in the Indo-Pacific, from Indonesia to the Great Barrier Reef. They grow up to 15 inches long and are known for their tall, sail-like dorsal fin.
With bold vertical stripes and vibrant yellow and blue markings, Sailfin Tangs graze on algae all day, helping keep coral reefs clean and balanced. A true beauty with a purpose!
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Moorish Idol! a stunning tropical fish found in waters from East Africa to Hawaii. With a flat, disc-shaped body up to 9 inches long, they love hanging around coral reefs.
They feed on sponges, coral polyps, and small invertebrates. Look for their bold black, white, and yellow stripes - and that long, trailing dorsal fin that makes them truly unforgettable!
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
Lionfish, introduced from the Indo-Pacific, have spread rapidly throughout the Western Atlantic. These beautiful predators grow up to 19 inches long and are defended by 18 venomous spines.
Lionfish devour anything they can catch - small fish, shrimp, crabs and more. With no natural predators in their new range, they’re a serious threat to native marine life and reef ecosystems.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
Meet the Rock Beauty Angelfish, a bold splash of color in the tropical western Atlantic—from Bermuda to Brazil. These beauties grow up to 8 inches long, hiding among coral and rocky ledges.
Their bright yellow bodies and deep black midsection make them easy to spot. Rock Beauties are omnivores, munching on sponges, algae, and small invertebrates as they cruise the reef with style.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.

Juvenile Queen Angelfish are found in the warm waters of the western Atlantic and Caribbean. At up to 8 inches long, they’re known for their deep blue bodies with bright yellow highlights and electric blue rings.
When young, they feed on sponges, algae, and small invertebrates, and also clean other fish. Darker colors help them blend in before they mature into dazzling blue and yellow adults.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.

The Yellowtail Snapper is a vibrant reef fish found throughout the Caribbean and western Atlantic. They’re easily recognized by their bright yellow stripe running from nose to tail, and their sleek, silver-blue body.
They grow up to 30 inches long and feed on small fish, shrimp, and crabs. Quick and curious, Yellowtails are key players in reef ecosystems, helping keep the balance beneath the waves

The Picasso Triggerfish, a bold Indo-Pacific beauty! Up to 10 inches long, this fish is named for its wild, painterly markings - blues, yellows, and blacks - art in motion.
It’s an aggressive little predator, feeding on crustaceans, sea urchins, and small fish. With a tough attitude and a face to match, it truly lives up to its artistic name!

In warm Western Atlantic waters, Sergeant Majors are easy to spot with bold black stripes over a silver-yellow body.  At up to 6 inches, they often swim in large schools near reefs and shipwrecks.
Sergeant Majors feast on algae, plankton, and invertebrates. Their name comes from stripes that look like a military insignia. They swirl and flash in unison—nature’s underwater choreography.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Tricolor Nudibranch is a small, vibrant marine creature found in tropical Indo-Pacific waters. Growing up to two inches long, it thrives on coral reefs and rocky seafloor habitats.

This nudibranch feeds on sponges and small invertebrates. Its striking colors—bright white, orange, and black—serve as a warning to predators about its toxicity and adds flair to its colorful surroundings.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.

Crabs are found in coastal tide pools, sandy beaches, and rocky shores around the world. They range in size from tiny to around 12 inches across. Their sturdy shells protect them from pounding waves and predators.

These crabs are scavengers, feeding on algae, small fish, and detritus. Many species have distinct markings—spots, stripes, or camouflage—that help them blend into their surroundings and avoid predators.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.
The Megalodon was a massive prehistoric shark that roamed warm oceans around the world. At 50 feet - over three times longer than a great white - it was one of the largest predators to ever live.
Megalodons fed on whales, dolphins, and large fish, using jaws packed with serrated teeth. Their markings likely included a pale belly and a darker back, perfect for ambushing prey from below.

The Pyjama Shark is found off South Africa, mostly in kelp forests and rocky reefs. It grows up to 3 feet long and gets its name from the bold, dark body stripes that look like pajama patterns.
This shark hunts at night, feeding on small fish, crustaceans, and squid. It’s a slow swimmer, but uses stealth and suction to snatch prey out of crevices and off the ocean floor.

The Short-Beaked Common Dolphin is found in tropical, and temperate waters around the world. They grow up to 8 feet long and are known for their sleek bodies and a striking hourglass pattern on their sides.
These dolphins feed on schooling fish like sardines and anchovies, often working together in large groups. Fast and playful, they’re one of the most social dolphin species in the ocean.

The Vaquita is the world’s rarest marine mammal, found only in Mexico’s northern Gulf of California. These shy porpoises grow up to five feet long and weigh around 100 pounds—small and sleek for a dolphin relative.

Vaquitas feed on fish, squid, and crustaceans in shallow water. They have dark eye rings and lip markings - like natural face paint - but sadly, they’re critically endangered, with fewer than 10 left.

The Sperm Whale and her calf glide through the deep waters of the world’s oceans, from the tropics to polar regions. Adults can reach up to 60 feet long, while calves start out at about 13 feet at birth.
They dive deep to hunt giant squid, using powerful clicks to echolocate prey. Sperm whales have wrinkled, gray skin and an iconic block-shaped head. Calves stay close, learning with every dive.

The Great White Shark is found in cool, coastal waters around the world, in both the Atlantic and Pacific oceans. Growing up to 20 feet long, it’s one of the largest predatory fish on Earth.
They feed on seals, fish, and even small whales. Their grey tops help them blend into the ocean depths, while their white bellies hide them from below - perfect camouflage for a powerful hunter.

The Green Moray Eel lives in the warm waters of the western Atlantic, from New Jersey to Brazil. It can grow up to eight feet long and has a thick, muscular body that helps it navigate rocky reefs and crevices.
Despite its name, it’s not actually green - its yellow skin is covered in blue mucus, giving it that eerie color. It feeds on fish, crabs, and squid, using sharp teeth and a powerful bite.

The Blue Tang is a vibrant reef fish in the warm waters of the Caribbean and Indo-Pacific. They grow up to 12 inches long and are easily recognized by their bright blue bodies and bold yellow tail.
Blue Tangs graze on algae, helping keep coral reefs healthy. Their oval shape and sharp spine near the tail make them agile and well-defended in the wild.

The Threadfin Butterflyfish is a tropical reef dweller found in the Indo-Pacific from the Red Sea to Hawaii. It grows up to 9 inches long and is known for its striking yellow body and bold black eye stripe.
It feeds on coral polyps, small invertebrates, and algae. A dark spot near its tail mimics an eye, helping to confuse predators while it slips through the coral with ease.

The Giant Trevally is a powerful predator in tropical waters from the Indian Ocean to the central Pacific. They can be over five feet long and weigh 175 pounds, making them one of the largest jacks in the ocean.

They hunt fish, crustaceans, and even birds near the surface. With silver bodies, dark dorsal area, and occasional black spots, they’re built for stealth and speed in reef and open water environments.

The Yellow Tang is a bright, tropical fish native to the reefs of Hawaii and parts of the Pacific Ocean. They grow up to eight inches long and are known for their bold, lemon-yellow color that stands out in coral habitats

The Powder Blue Tang is a vibrant reef fish found in the warm waters of the Indian Ocean. It grows up to 9 inches long and is known for its electric blue body, black face mask, and bright yellow dorsal fin.
This active swimmer feeds mainly on algae, helping to keep coral reefs healthy. It thrives in shallow lagoons and reef slopes, darting through coral crevices with speed and precision.

The Sea Nettle Jellyfish drifts through Atlantic and Pacific coastal waters. With long, flowing tentacles and a bell that can grow up to 20 inches wide, it’s both graceful and powerful in the water.

Sea Nettles feed on zooplankton, small fish, and even other jellies. Look for their golden-brown bell, often marked with radiating stripes - an unmistakable pattern that sets them apart beneath the waves.

The Compass Jellyfish drifts through the coastal waters of the UK and parts of the Mediterranean. Growing up to 12 inches across, it has a bell marked with brown, V-shaped lines that make it look like a compass.

The Double Saddle Butterflyfish lives in the tropical waters of the Indo-Pacific, from Hawaii to the coasts of Indonesia. These reef fish grow up to 7 inches long and often swim in pairs in coral-rich habitats.

The Copperband Butterflyfish is a striking reef dweller in the Indo-Pacific, from Australia to Southeast Asia. These graceful swimmers grow up to 8 inches long and glide through coral reefs with ease.

Meet the Fusilier, a sleek reef fish found across the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea to Australia. They average around 12 inches long and move in fast, shimmering schools that light up the coral reefs.
Fusiliers feed on zooplankton, darting through the water with quick, synchronized bursts. Look for their bright blue or silver bodies, often with yellow or black stripes running down their sides.

The Clown Triggerfish lives in warm Indo-Pacific waters, from the coast of Africa to Polynesia. Growing up to 20 inches long, it’s known for its bold personality and striking appearance.
With a jet-black body, white spots, and bright yellow lips, it stands out on the reef. It feeds on sea urchins, crustaceans, and mollusks—crushing them with strong, sharp teeth.

Thresher Sharks are found in temperate and tropical oceans around the world. These sleek predators can grow up to 20 feet long, with nearly half that length coming from their unique, whip-like tail.
Thresher Sharks use that long tail to stun schools of fish before feeding. With big eyes, a pointed snout, and a grayish top fading to a white belly, they’re built for speed, stealth, and surprise.

This is the Green Sea Turtle—found in tropical and subtropical oceans around the world. These gentle giants can grow up to 5 feet long and weigh over 400 pounds!
They graze on seagrass and algae, helping keep ocean habitats healthy. Look for their smooth, heart-shaped shells with patterns of browns and olive green.

Meet the ocean’s gentle giant - the Sunfish, or Mola mola! Found in warm and temperate oceans worldwide, these odd-looking fish can grow over 10 feet tall and weigh more than 2,000 pounds!

Sunfish feed mostly on jellyfish, squid, and small fish. Their flat, round bodies are silver-gray, with rough skin and distinct fins that stick straight up and down, making them look like swimming dinner plates!

Cruising the tropical seas from Hawaii to the Maldives, majestic Manta Rays can have wingspans up to 23 feet. These gentle giants filter plankton and krill through their gills as they swim.

Each manta has unique belly spot patterns - like a fingerprint - that can be used to identify them. With horn-shaped fins and graceful movements, they’re one of the ocean’s most captivating and mysterious creatures.

The Percula Clownfish is bright orange with black-lined white stripes. From the warm Indo-Pacific, especially near coral reefs around Papua New Guinea and Australia, these fish grow up to 4 inches long.

Meet the Black Percula Clownfish, from the warm reefs of Papua New Guinea and northern Australia. They grow up to 4 inches long and are known for their bold black coloring with bright white bands.

These little reef dwellers are omnivores, feeding on algae, plankton, and small invertebrates. Their vibrant contrast and wobbly movement make them one of the ocean’s most recognizable and beloved fish.

The mesmerizing Moon Jellyfish glides through oceans around the world. They’re up to 16 inches wide, with clear, saucer-shaped bodies with four distinct horseshoe-shaped gonads at the center.

The Emperor Angelfish lives in warm reef waters across the Indo-Pacific, from the Red Sea to Hawaii. Up to 15 inches long, it’s known for its striking blue and yellow stripes with a bold black mask over its eyes.

Meet the Flame Angelfish — from tropical Pacific reefs, especially around the Marshall, Line, and Christmas Islands, these stunning fish grow up to 4 inches long and add serious color to coral reefs.
They feed on algae and small invertebrates, helping balance reef ecosystems. Their bold orange-red bodies, black bars, and electric blue fin edging make them a standout in any underwater scene.

The Regal Angelfish lives in coral-rich waters from the Red Sea to the Indo-Pacific. It grows up to 10 inches long and prefers sheltered reefs, where it weaves through crevices with ease.
Its vivid blue and yellow stripes, with a black-edged orange tail, make it stand out. Regal Angelfish feed on sponges and tunicates, playing a key role in keeping reef ecosystems balanced.

Longnose Butterflyfish are found on tropical reefs across the Indo-Pacific. These small, elegant fish grow up to 9 inches long and are known for their long, narrow snouts used to reach food in tight spaces.
They feed on coral polyps, tiny crustaceans, and worms. With bright yellow bodies, black eye bands, and white faces, their bold markings help them blend into the reef while confusing predators.

The Hammerhead Shark is found in warm waters around the world, especially near North & Central America, Africa, and Australia. They grow up to 20 feet long and are known for their wide, flat hammer-shaped heads.

They feed on stingrays, fish, and squid, using their head to pin prey to the seafloor. With gray-brown coloring on top and a pale underside, their body helps them blend into the sea from above and below.

Nurse Sharks are slow-moving bottom-dwellers in warm Atlantic and eastern Pacific waters, often on coral reefs or in mangroves. Adults can grow to 10 feet, with broad heads and whisker-like barbels near the mouth.
They feed mostly at night on fish, crustaceans, and mollusks on the sea floor. They’re usually brownish with smooth skin and sometimes faint spots, helping them blend into sandy or rocky habitats.

The Tahitian Butterflyfish is a small reef dweller in the tropical waters of French Polynesia. Reaching about 5 inches in length, it glides through coral gardens with ease, always staying close to shelter.

It feeds on coral polyps and invertebrates, reaching its long snout into tight spaces. Black stripes on a white and yellow body make it easy to spot - yet it still manages to blend into the reef's vibrant chaos.

The Speckled Butterflyfish lives in the warm waters of the Indo-Pacific, often near coral reefs. It grows up to five inches long, with a rounded body built for slipping through tight reef spaces.
This fish feeds mostly on coral polyps and small invertebrates. Its pale body is covered in black speckles, with a dark band through the eye and a bold spot near the tail to confuse predators.

The Pearlscale Butterflyfish is found in the Indo-Pacific, from Japan to the Great Barrier Reef. It grows to about 6 inches long and lives in coral-rich lagoons and on outer reef slopes.
It feeds mostly on algae and coral polyps. Its body is white with a network of dark lines, a bright orange band across its face, and a black spot near the tail to confuse predators.

The Spotband Butterflyfish lives on shallow Indo-Pacific reefs from Indonesia to the Great Barrier Reef. Up to five inches long, its flat, oval body helps it slip easily through coral branches.
Its black bands and yellow tail make it easy to spot. It feeds on coral polyps, algae, and small invertebrates, using its narrow snout to reach into tight spaces. A specialist with style.

The Millet Butterflyfish is found in the tropical Indo-Pacific, especially around coral reefs. They grow up to 7 inches long and are known for their bright yellow bodies and small black spots.
These fish feed on coral polyps, algae, and small invertebrates. Their striking markings include a dark band over the eye and a spot near the tail, helping them blend in and confuse predators.

The Striped Sweetlips is found in the warm waters of the Indo-Pacific, especially around coral reefs in Indonesia and the Maldives. They can grow up to 3 feet long, moving in slow, graceful schools near the reef.
These fish feed on crustaceans, small fish, and invertebrates. Their bold yellow-and-black stripes and spotted fins help them blend into reef shadows, offering camouflage from predators.

The Ribbon Sweetlips lives on the warm coral reefs of the Indo-Pacific, from the Maldives to the Great Barrier Reef. As juveniles, they’re small and slender, but adults can grow up to 30 inches long.

They feed on crustaceans and small fish, often hunting at night. Their bold yellow stripes and blue-gray bodies stand out, while their thick lips and undulating fins give them a signature, flowing look.
If you’re still curious, check your Dive Log for more information.

The Oblique Banded Sweetlips is found in warm Indo-Pacific waters from the Maldives to Papua New Guinea. These striking reef fish grow up to 30 inches long and often swim in loose groups around coral reefs.
They feed mainly on small invertebrates and crustaceans at night. Their bold, slanted stripes and large, fleshy lips make them easy to spot, especially as their colors become more vibrant with age.

The Striped Tang, also known as the Clown Surgeonfish, is found in the Indo-Pacific region. It can grow up to 15 inches long and is known for its bold horizontal blue and yellow stripes that run across its body.
This active swimmer feeds mainly on algae, helping keep coral reefs clean and balanced. With its unique markings and important role in the ecosystem, the Striped Tang is a true standout on the reef.

The Convict Tang is a reef fish named for its bold black stripes—like prison bars—across a silvery-white body. Found in the Indo-Pacific, they love warm, shallow reefs and are often seen swimming in large schools.
These active grazers grow up to 6 inches long and spend their days munching on algae. Their constant grazing helps keep reefs clean and healthy, making them an important part of the ocean ecosystem!

Meet Dive Xtras underwater scooters - perfect for exploring large wrecks and reefs, hunting lionfish, and more.
Lionfish hunters, like Patrick in Aruba, move rapidly between hotspots without wasting air, energy or valuable bottom time, maximizing removals on every dive.
These Gold Standard scooters are designed to minimize noise, making it easier for hunters to approach lionfish without spooking them.
Dive Xtras scooters are perfect for targeting hard-to-reach populations, and directly contribute to reducing the spread and impact of invasive lionfish.
In fact, we used Dive Xtras scooters to create the high-quality, hyper-realistic scans of all the wrecks featured in Shipwreck Discovery!
Powerful Performance & Deep Rating: The BlackTip Tech delivers over 57 lb of thrust and reaches boost‑mode speeds up to ~238 feet per minute, making quick work of currents.

Excellent Range & Battery Flexibility: Using dual 9Ah or 12Ah DCB (power‑tool style) batteries gives impressive cruise runtimes (≈ 90 minutes with 9Ah, up to ~123 minutes with 12Ah) and ranges of over 4‑5 miles at cruise speeds.
Neutral Trim & Buoyancy Control: Right out of the box the Tech model floats trim (level) and neutral in water when the proper batteries are used.

User‑Friendly Controls & Safety Features: The BlackTip Tech has clever trigger‑based controls: double‑click to start, double‑click to shift up, single click to slow, triple click for a “jump speed,” and even reverse / “untangle” speeds. alarms, etc., are customizable via a Bluetooth app.

RMS Atlantis - a 125-foot-long oil field service vessel built in 1979. She proudly carried the Royal Mail designation for the British postal service.
Working in the oil fields of the North Sea, Atlantis likely delivered mail and supplies to offshore rigs, possibly for BP or another major British oil company.
After decades of service, she was retired and prepared for a new purpose - becoming an artificial reef to support marine life and ocean conservation efforts.
On June 29, 2023, RMS Atlantis was intentionally sunk to her final resting place, becoming part of a growing reef system beneath the waves.
She now sits upright on a sandy seabed at a depth of 80 feet, making her an accessible and exciting dive site for recreational divers.
With the top of her wheelhouse at just 52 feet, divers can explore her structure, enjoy marine life, and witness history blending with the reef.
Welcome to the BIG DAWG. once a training vessel for the U.S. Air Force Parachute Water Survival School, and now an exciting dive site with a distinguished history.
This 93-foot vessel was donated by the Air Force and transformed into an artificial reef, deployed in May 2021 off the coast of Florida.
She now rests upright on a sandy seabed at a depth of 104 feet, or 32 meters, making her an accessible and thrilling challenge for advanced divers.
BIG DAWG provides nearly 30 feet of vertical relief, with multiple levels to explore, including areas that mimic the ship's original structure and layout.
Before deployment, large holes were cut into the deck to give divers access to unique interior spaces like the mess hall, classroom, and living quarters.
This artificial reef isn’t just a dive—it’s an underwater experience that connects history, marine conservation, and exploration in one, unforgettable adventure.
The M/V Brannon was sunk as an artificial reef on July 29, 2021—less than a year after the nearby S/V Dylan was sunk. These two wrecks now sit, just a few feet apart.
Originally a 65-foot, steel-hulled military catamaran, Brannon was a fast-response experimental vessel before it found its final resting place beneath the waves.
Located 16 miles off the coast of Destin–Fort Walton Beach in 119 feet of water, this wreck is part of the Okaloosa County Artificial Reef Program and lies at a diveable depth.
The ship rests upright on the seafloor. The aft section of its superstructure has collapsed, with its upper deck now resting in the sand near the starboard stern.
Despite the partial collapse, Brannon offers an impressive 14 feet—or 4 meters—of vertical relief, making it an excellent spot for exploration.
Today, the wreck is a thriving haven for marine life and wreck divers - a living reef and underwater attraction.
Dylan, a 61-foot sailing vessel, was caught smuggling 400 pounds of cocaine. In its hull, a $7.5 million secret. Seized in Martinique where its dark journey began
December 2019, Port Everglades. Customs agents uncover 189 kilos of cocaine stashed in Dylan's steel hull. Seized and forfeited, its fate was sealed.
Gifted to Okaloosa County, Dylan sailed her final voyage to Destin, Florida. Cleaned and prepped, it was ready for a new purpose - under the sea.
In 2020, Dylan was sunk 16 miles southeast of Destin’s East Pass. Morgan Marine deployed it to become an artificial reef, a haven for marine life.
Now 120 feet down, Dylan rests a few feet from another reefed ship - the Brannon. The location is called  ‘the boatyard’ where these and other shipwrecks wait to be explored.
Once a smuggler’s ship, Dylan now thrives as an artificial reef, teeming with life. A new legacy awaits off Destin’s coast.
Meet the Miss Joann, a 95-foot steel tugboat that worked for decades in the Gulf of Mexico before being retired and prepared for a new life - underwater
On February 13, 2013, Miss Joann was intentionally sunk, as part of Okaloosa County’s artificial reef program, to boost marine life and attract divers.
Miss Joann rests in the Gulf about 16 nautical miles south of Destin, Florida, in a popular area called - the Boatyard.
She sits upright on a sandy bottom 110 feet deep, with the highest point of the wreck at around 80 feet - ideal for advanced divers.
Since her sinking, Miss Joann has become a thriving reef, now home to amberjack, snapper, grouper, and even the occasional sea turtle and shark.
The upright structure and easy access points make Miss Joann a favorite among wreck divers and underwater photographers exploring Florida’s artificial reefs.
In 1982, the 62-foot tugboat Miss Nellie was built. Designed for heavy-duty towing, it was used for decades in Panama City, Florida, before being decommissioned in 2022.
In spring 2022, Miss Nellie was donated to Okaloosa County. The goal? Transform the tugboat into an artificial reef to boost marine life and diving tourism off Destin’s coast.
Before sinking, Miss Nellie was stripped of non-metallic parts to ensure environmental safety. Okaloosa County’s Coastal Resource Team cleaned the vessel, readying it for its new role as a reef.
On August 23, 2022, the 62-foot tug was sunk 21 nautical miles southeast of Destin. She settled upright in 112 feet of water, creating a new artificial reef.
After sinking, coastal resource divers dove to inspect Miss Nellie and found the tugboat landed perfectly upright, in great condition, ready to host fish and divers.
Today, Miss Nellie is a thriving artificial reef, fostering marine life and attracting divers and adventure seekers from all over the world.
